coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional cerium doped carbon dots nanoplatform and its applications for wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mengzhen
Zhai, Xinyun
Ma, Tengfei
Huang, Yongkang
Yan, Chunhua
Du, Yaping
coredata.dc:description: Oxidative stress, which is induced by the overproduced free radicals around inflammation, is one of the most important factors to delay the wound healing process. Much efforts have been focused on wound repair with an emphasis on exploring novel biomaterials which have prominent antioxidant activity and good biocompatibility. In this work, cerium-doped carbon nanodots (Ce-CNDs) are fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-prepared Ce-CNDs have monodispersed and ultrasmall sizes ranging from 2 to 4 nm with unique photoluminescence, good photostability and certain antibacterial property. MTT assays reveal that Ce-CNDs exhibit superior biocompatibility, and the nanoscale of Ce-CNDs is very helpful for the penetration of cell membranes for cell imaging. The most remarkable point is that the coexistence of Ce(III)/Ce(IV) in Ce-CNDs endows them with attractive antioxidant property to resist the excessive inflammation and accelerate the wound healing process thereby. In consequence, this finding explores the beneficial effect of Ce-CNDs in wound healing for the first time and develops the potential applications of Ce-CNDs as visualization and treatment integrated nanoplatform in oxidative-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cerium
Carbon nanodots
Antioxidant
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Apoptosis induction and cell viability of Ce-CNDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China [grant number 2017YFA0208000 ]; National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 21971117 , 21771156 , 52003124 ]; China Postdoctoral Science Foundation [grant number 2020M680862 ]; the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Nankai University [grant numbers 63211092 , ZB19500202 ]; State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization [grant number RERU2019001 ]; the 111 Project from China [grant number B18030 ]; and Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Collaborative Innovation Project [ 63201058 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) TEM images of Ce-CNDs. (c) HRTEM image of Ce-CNDs. (d) The size distribution of dialyzed Ce-CNDs. (e) The FT-IR spectrum of Ce-CNDs and CNDs. (f) XPS analysis of Ce3d spectrum.

(a) UV–vis spectrum and PL emission spectrum (λex = 359 nm) of Ce-CNDs. (b) Excitation spectrum (λem = 433 nm) and PL emission spectrum (λex = 359 nm) of Ce-CNDs. (c) PL emission spectra of Ce-CNDs with different excitation wavelengths. (d) Normalized PL spectra of Ce-CNDs. (the inset is the variation of emission wavelengths at 80% of the normalized PL intensity upon different excitation wavelengths).

(a) Cell viability of L929 cells versus different incubated concentrations of Ce-CNDs. (b) Cell apoptosis of L929 cells induced by different concentrations of H2O2. (c) Cell viability of L929 cells incubated with different concentrations of Ce-CNDs under 300 µM H2O2 -induced oxidative stress. (d) Remaining scratch area of wound scratch. (e) Representative optical images of in vitro wound scratch assay on L929 cells treated with PBS, CNDs, or Ce-CNDs at increasing incubation times (scale bar: 500 μm). Data are expressed as mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.
Fluorescent inverted microscopic images of L929 cells (a) labeled with 800 µg mL−1 Ce-CNDs and (b) without labeling as a negative control.

(a) Representative photographs of full-thickness excision wounds at different time nodes upon treatment with PBS, CNDs, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O or Ce-CNDs. (b) Wound healing rates (%) of PBS, CNDs, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs treated groups on days 3, 5, 8, 10 and 15 post-wounding. (c) Wound remaining (%) of PBS, CNDs, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs treated groups on days 3, 5, 8, 10 and 15 post-wounding. Data are expressed as mean ± SD, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

Representative images of H&E stained histopathological sections of healing tissues of PBS, CNDs, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs treated groups on days 3, 5, 8, 10 and 15 post-wounding (scale bar: 100 μm). Inflammatory cells, black arrow; blood vessels, blue circle.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process and applications of Ce-CNDs.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional cerium doped carbon dots nanoplatform and its applications for wound healing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Lab for Rare Earth Materials and Applications
Center for Rare Earth and Inorganic Functional Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering & National Institute for Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidative stress, which is induced by the overproduced free radicals around inflammation, is one of the most important factors to delay the wound healing process. Much efforts have been focused on wound repair with an emphasis on exploring novel biomaterials which have prominent antioxidant activity and good biocompatibility. In this work, cerium-doped carbon nanodots (Ce-CNDs) are fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. The as-prepared Ce-CNDs have monodispersed and ultrasmall sizes ranging from 2 to 4 nm with unique photoluminescence, good photostability and certain antibacterial property. MTT assays reveal that Ce-CNDs exhibit superior biocompatibility, and the nanoscale of Ce-CNDs is very helpful for the penetration of cell membranes for cell imaging. The most remarkable point is that the coexistence of Ce(III)/Ce(IV) in Ce-CNDs endows them with attractive antioxidant property to resist the excessive inflammation and accelerate the wound healing process thereby. In consequence, this finding explores the beneficial effect of Ce-CNDs in wound healing for the first time and develops the potential applications of Ce-CNDs as visualization and treatment integrated nanoplatform in oxidative-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: In general, wound healing process can be arbitrarily divided into four temporary overlapping procedures: hemostasis, inflammation, cell proliferation, and extracellular matrix remodeling [1,2]. Hemostasis is the first stage occurred immediately after skin injury. Then the repair process undergoes the inflammatory stage to prevent the wound from infections, clears out damaged cells and other debris through phagocytosis, which effectively inhibits bacteria growth and accelerates the wound healing process [3,4]. However, in order to protect wounds against infection, excessive inflammation occurs frequently around the wounds, which can cause the overproduction of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and induce oxidative stress [5,6]. Such oxidative stress in vivo induces cell oxidative injury and impairs cell proliferation, and finally delays the wound healing process or even causes extensive damage to the wound sites. Therefore, introducing antioxidant agents which can eliminate ROS efficiently into wound repair materials is an effective way to drive wounds from inflammatory phase to cell proliferation phase, and finally accelerate wound healing.

Among the 17 rare earth elements, cerium has the most abundant reserves and is widely used in engineering materials, such as polishing agents, catalyst supports, fuel cell electrolytes, etc. [7–11]. Recently, cerium has attracted considerable attention in biomedical field by virtue of their excellent antioxidant properties. Cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2 NPs), for example, have fluorite crystal structure with abundant oxygen vacancies resulting from the lattice defects. The formation and migration of oxygen vacancies lead to the reversible conversion between Ce(III) and Ce(IV) in CeO2 NPs, which is the primary reason for their superior ROS scavenging and versatile enzyme-mimetic properties, including superoxide dismutase (SOD) [12], catalase (CAT) [13,14], hydroxy radical scavenging activity [15], etc. These enzyme-like properties indicate the significant potential of CeO2 NPs in the treatment of oxidative stress-related diseases, such as cancer [16,17], retinal damage [18], diabetic complications [19], chronic inflammatory [20] and as such. By virtue of their antioxidant properties, CeO2 NPs have already been reported to accelerate the cutaneous wound healing by alleviating the inflammatory stage and enhancing the proliferation and migration of major skin forming cells [21–24]. However, compared with the form of oxide which can only disperse in water to generate a colloid solution [14], the hydrophilic cerium-based materials have better biocompatibility and applicability to the skin wound, which have greater potential in clinical use.

Carbon materials, one of the most widely used natural materials, have gained tremendous impact on the scientific research, with expansion of their applications in healthcare area in recent years [25–27]. For decades, the development of nanofabrication techniques has accelerated the boom of carbon-based nanomaterials, including fullerene, carbon nanotube, graphene, carbon nanodots etc. [26,28,29]. These carbon nanomaterials have many unique electrical, optical, magnetic and chemical properties owing to the quantum confinement effect, which have aroused a great attention in the past years [27]. Among them, the zero-dimensional carbon nanodots (CNDs) belong to the next generation of carbon materials and have been extensively investigated since the discovery in 2004 [30]. For decades, CNDs are fascinating researchers for their high biocompatibility, excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties and excellent photostability, and have been found applications in a variety of domains, including energy storage and conversion [31], photocatalysis [32], electroanalytical methods [33], electrochemiluminescence [34], biomedicine [35–41], etc. The versatile and modifiable functional groups on the surface of CNDs impart them with special designability and tunable features, making them superior nanocarriers in biological systems. The new emerging rare earth-doped carbon nanodots (RE-CNDs) are inspiring intensive research efforts in recent years, for they combine several favorable attributes from both rare earth and CNDs [42,43]. RE-CNDs take advantage of the great hydrophilicity and optical properties of CNDs and the characteristics of rare earth, making them suitable candidates for multifunctional nanomaterials in biomedical field [44–47]. Therefore, we hypothesis that the combination of cerium and CNDs not only constructs a multifunctional nanoplatform with both superior hydrophilicity and bioimaging properties, but also provides an effective agent with antioxidant activity, which is promising for biomedical engineering.

In this work, cerium element and CNDs are integrated into a single nanoplatform to form cerium-doped carbon nanodots (Ce-CNDs) by a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Efforts have been made to explore the physical and optical properties of Ce-CNDs. Besides good PL properties, Ce-CNDs also exhibit certain antibacterial capability under UV irradiation. Catalase mimetic activity assays and in vivo wound healing models were conducted to evaluate the antioxidant properties of Ce-CNDs. Owing to the antioxidant activity, superior hydrophilicity and good biocompatibility of Ce-CNDs, they can be easily employed to the cutaneous wound healing by wiping or spraying. Moreover, based on all the specialities, Ce-CNDs exhibit huge potential for the visualization and treatment of oxidative-related diseases as a multifunctional nanoplatform.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.5%) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 35 wt%) were purchased from Innochem Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA, >99%) and paraformaldehyde were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), streptomycin and penicillin were procured from GIBCO BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). Trypticase soy broth (TSB), LB nutrient agar and phosphate buffered saline powder (PBS) were obtained from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Chloral hydrate was purchased from Hwrk Chemical Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Water used in relevant experiments was ultrapure water (Thermo Scientific, GenPure UV/UF, Germany). Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were obtained from the School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University (Tianjin, China), and stored at −20 °C until used.

Ce-CNDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 0.800 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 1.062 g CA were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (DI water) and stirred for 1 h. Then 1.11 mL EDA was subsequently added to the above solution. After mixed uniformly, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the solution was purified by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 15 min to remove the white impurities. The prepared transparent brown solution was then transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1.0 kDa) and dialyzed for 5 days to remove the unreacted organic molecules. The dialysate was collected and lyophilized with vacuum freeze dryer for 48 h. The obtained Ce-CNDs powders were weighted quickly and stored for further characterization. As a control, the undoped CNDs were synthesized under similar conditions except adding Ce(NO3)3·6H2O in reactants.

The morphology and size distribution of Ce-CNDs were detected using the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) analyses performed on a JEM-2800 microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The detailed microscopic structure was analyzed using the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operated at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was recorded on a Bruker Tensor 37 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hʋ = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source (Thermal Scientific, USA). Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired by a Shimadzu UV-2600 UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The PL spectra were characterized by a Techcomp FL970 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Techcomp, China).

S. aureus were used to assess the photodynamical antibacterial activity of Ce-CNDs by spread plate method. Briefly, S. aureus were firstly cultured in LB medium at 37 °C overnight. Then 100 µL of S. aureus were seeded into a 24-well plate and diluted to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1. After that, 100 µL of Ce-CNDs solution were added into the diluted bacteria suspensions (900 µL) with different final concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400 and 800 µg mL−1) and kept in a shaker with 160 rpm at 37 °C for 5 min. The blank group was pure bacteria without incubation with Ce-CNDs. The bacterial solutions were exposed to UV light (254 nm, 1.2 W cm−2) in a UV-cross-linker (XL-1000, Spectronics, USA) for 5 min, while the same samples treated without irradiation were set as the control groups. Subsequently, 100 µL of diluted bacteria solutions (104 times diluted by PBS) were spread onto the agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. All experimental groups were measured in triplicate.

The morphology change of bacteria was further characterized by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) observation which were recorded on a JSM-7800F microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan), operating at an acceleration voltage of 0.01 to 30 kV. For FE-SEM analysis, all the groups with or without UV irradiation were then washed with PBS twice after incubation, and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 4 h. Thereafter, the samples were washed with PBS for three times to remove the paraformaldehyde, and dehydrated in gradient with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80% and 90% (v/v) ethanol solutions for 15 min respectively, and then washed with anhydrous ethanol twice for 15 min. The dehydrated bacteria were dried at 60 °C overnight. Finally, the treated bacteria were adhered to the conductive tap and sputter-coated with gold for FE-SEM observation.

Mouse fibroblast cell line L929 (provided by School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin University, Tianjin, China) was maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 100 µg mL−1 streptomycin and 100 U mL−1 penicillin at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2. The medium was changed every 2 days, and the cells were digested by trypsin and re-dispersed in fresh medium before confluence.

MTT assays were employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared Ce-CNDs using L929 cells. Briefly, L929 cells were seeded into a 96-well plate at the concentration of 1.5 × 104 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Then Ce-CNDs were added into the 96-well plate with different final concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 and 1600 µg mL−1). After incubation for 24 h with the addition of Ce-CNDs, the cells were washed with PBS. 90 µL fresh culture medium and 10 µL MTT solution (dissolved in PBS, 5 mg mL−1) were added to each well, and the 96-well plate was incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. Subsequently, the supernatant was removed and 100 µL of DMSO was added to each well followed by shaken for 10 min. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured on a microplate reader (Thermofisher Varioskan LUX, Finland). In addition, the cytotoxicity of Ce(III) was tested by the same MTT method with Ce(NO3)3·6H2O as the source of Ce(III). The concentrations of Ce(III) were set to be 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100 and 500 µg mL−1. All experiments were carried out in sextuplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Apoptosis induction and cell viability of Ce-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The H2O2 induced cell apoptosis can be a valuable model for the study of oxidative injury. In our study, the protective effect of Ce-CNDs against the oxidative stress in vitro were tested using diluted H2O2 to induce apoptosis of L929 cells. As described in the above steps, L929 cells were initially seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 1.5 × 104 cells per well and pre-incubated for 24 h. Then cells were co-incubated with Ce-CNDs of different final concentrations (0, 20, 40, 50 and 60 µg mL−1) for another 24 h·H2O2 solution was freshly diluted from a 35 wt% stock solution, and then 3 µL of H2O2 solution (10 mM) was directly added to each well to make a final concentration of 300 µM H2O2 exposure. After 2 h of incubation, cells were washed with PBS and the MTT assay was conducted for the determination of cell viability as described in the last section.

The influence of the concentration of H2O2 solution on the apoptosis of L929 cells was also conducted. The experimental procedure was the same as above without the co-incubation with Ce-CNDs. All experiments were carried out in sextuplicate.

L929 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and grown in DMEM to confluence. Then scratches were made onto the cells by scrapping the cell monolayers with a sterile 10 µL pipet tip. After gentle rinsing with PBS to remove the floating cells, Ce-CNDs or CNDs with the final concentration of 50 µg mL−1 were added and incubated in a CO2 incubator (37 °C, 5% CO2 supply) for serum starvation (DMEM supplemented with 1% FBS). The images of the scratches were taken at 0, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h of incubation time using the Leica inverted fluorescence microscope, and the lesion areas were calculated using Image J software.

The distribution and cellular uptake of the Ce-CNDs in L929 cells were visualized by the Leica inverted fluorescence microscope. For fluorescent imaging observation, L929 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and incubated for 24 h until spread out completely. Then cells were co-incubated with Ce-CNDs at the concentration of 800 µg mL−1 for another 24 h. After thoroughly washed with PBS, the fluorescent imaging of cells was observed. The fluorescent microscopy images were taken under bright field, UV (340–380 nm), blue (460–500 nm) and green (517–563 nm) laser excitation, respectively.

Sixteen 8-week-old Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (180–210 g) were provided by Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The rats were housed in an SPF-class laboratory in stainless steel cages with free access to food and water. All experiments were approved by the Center of Tianjin Animal Experiment Ethics Committee and Authority for Animal Protection (Approval No. SYXK (Jin) 2019–0001).

The SD rats were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of chloral hydrate (7 wt%, diluted with ultrapure water). Hair on the dorsal surface of the skin was shaved aseptically. Four circular full thickness open excision wounds were created on the back of each rat with a sterile 16-mm-diameter biopsy punch. Four wounds on each rat were then treated with 20 µL of PBS, CNDs (50 µg mL−1), Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (50 µg mL−1), and Ce-CNDs (50 µg mL−1), respectively. On days 0, 3, 5, 8, 10 and 15, wounds were imaged and tissues around each wound were harvested for histological assessments after execution. The collected samples were preserved in 4% paraformaldehyde at 4 °C. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E, Solarbio, G1120) staining was conducted to assess the wound healing process. The wound areas were calculated by Image J software.

Both in vitro and in vivo experiments were analyzed by the un-paired Students’ t-test using OriginPro 9.0 and expressedas means ± standard deviations (SD). A p value < 0.05 was considered to be statistically significant.

The Ce-CNDs with both excellent hydrophilicity and antioxidant activity were fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. TEM images display the quasi-spherical morphology of the as-prepared Ce-CNDs with homogenous size distribution (Fig. 1 a, b). The average particle size of Ce-CNDs is estimated to be 2–4 nm (Fig. 1d). The HRTEM image reveals the crystalline nature of the as-prepared Ce-CNDs with the lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1e shows the FT-IR spectrum of Ce-CNDs and CNDs. The intense and broad band at around 3400 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 1380 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H bending vibration in –CH3 group. The bands at 1660 cm−1 and 1329 cm−1 are attributed to the C = O and C-N stretching vibration respectively, indicating the existence of –CONH- bonds in both CNDs and Ce-CNDs.

To unveil the Ce-doping status in Ce-CNDs, XPS analysis was conducted. The results indicate that the as-prepared Ce-CNDs are composed of four elements, which are C, N, O and Ce, indicating the successful doping of cerium (Fig. S1). The high resolution XPS spectrum of Ce 3d (Fig. 1f) indicates that the chemical valence of cerium in Ce-CNDs is a tervalence and tetravalence mixed states. The curves of Ce 3d can be separated into nine peaks corresponding to the Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 spin–orbit. The coexistence of Ce(III) and Ce(IV) provides evidence for the antioxidant property of Ce-CNDs.

The UV–vis and PL spectra shown in Fig. 2 illustrate the optical properties of the as-prepared Ce-CNDs. In Fig. 2a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a peak at around 240 nm and a shoulder appearing at around 348 nm, which belong to the π-π* transitions of aromatic C–C bonds in Ce-CNDs and the n-π* transitions of C = O bonds or other surface groups on Ce-CNDs, respectively [48,49]. As observed in Fig. 2b, the Ce-CNDs exhibit intensive PL property with the optimal emission intensity centered at 433 nm under the excitation wavelength of 359 nm. Although the emission intensity of Ce-CNDs increases initially and then decreases as the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 420 nm (Fig. 2c), the emission wavelengths of Ce-CNDs only show slight red shift of 10 nm as illustrated in the normalized PL spectrum (Fig. 2d). This result demonstrates the superior photostability of the as-prepared Ce-CNDs that the emission wavelength of Ce-CNDs is almost irrelevant to the excitation wavelength. Furthermore, both the UV–vis and PL spectra of Ce-CNDs are similar to the pure CNDs without doping with cerium, indicating that the introduction of cerium does not affect the optical performance of CNDs and can maintain the PL and excellent photostability of CNDs (Fig. S2).

It has been reported that CNDs are effective antibacterial agents in the novel nanomedicine with high biosecurity and broad-spectrum antibacterial activities [50–53]. Upon light irradiation, some CNDs generate ROS through the transfer of excitation energy to oxygen, which can kill microorganisms by damaging DNA, inactivating enzymes and oxidizing amino acids [54,55]. In our study, co-culture and spread plate method were conducted to test the antibacterial activity of Ce-CNDs under UV excitation. As prolonged UV exposure can also inhibit the growth of bacteria, we tested the influence of UV light (UV-cross-linker, 254 nm, 1.2 W cm−2) to S. Aureus at first (Fig. S3). Results show that the growth of S. Aureus is not influenced by UV light when the exposure time is <5 min which is further applied in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. S4a, the growth of S. Aureus is fairly inhibited in Ce-CNDs treated groups after exposing to UV light for 5 min, and the minimum effective concentration is 200 µg mL−1. On the contrary, in the absence of UV light, no inhibition to S. Aureus is observed both with or without the treatment of Ce-CNDs. FE-SEM images were taken to further visualize the antibacterial effect of Ce-CNDs (Fig. S4b). Before UV excitation, the S. Aureus cultured with Ce-CNDs show complete spherical shape with no apparent difference to the control group. After exposed to UV light, the bacterial cell walls are extremely damaged and the leakage of cellular contents exist in the groups treated with Ce-CNDs, while the shape and morphology of S. Aureus in the control group without treated with Ce-CNDs are still well maintained. The probable antibacterial mechanisms are speculated to be the ROS generation by Ce-CNDs upon UV excitation. The photosensitizing property of CNDs has attracted special attention for the production of ROS, which has been used in the treatment of cancer and the inactivation of bacteria [56–58]. The ROS can induce oxidative stress in microbials and cause severe damage to the organic constituents including DNA, RNA and proteins [59]. The results indicate the great potential of Ce-CNDs for antibacterial applications, while further explorations of the inherent mechanisms are still deserved.

The biocompatibility of cerium ions and the as-prepared Ce-CNDs is of great significance for the applications of Ce-CNDs in the treatment of oxidative stress-related diseases, such as cancer, neurological disorders, diabetes and chronic inflammation [16–20]. L929 cells were employed in our study to measure the cytotoxicity and the antioxidant properties of Ce(III) and Ce-CNDs in vitro. Firstly, we evaluated the cell viability of L929 cells when incubated with Ce(III) or Ce-CNDs at increasing concentrations. MTT assay reveals that compared with the control group, Ce(III) and Ce-CNDs exhibit better biocompatibility even if the concentration is as high as 500 µg mL−1 and 1600 µg mL−1 over the incubation time of 24 h, respectively (Fig. S5 and Fig. 3 a). The results indicate that the superior biocompatibility of CNDs is still well maintained after the introduction of cerium.

It is well-known that CeO2 NPs has excellent catalase mimetic property owing to the reversible conversion between Ce(III) and Ce(IV) [13]. We speculate that the as-prepared Ce-CNDs with mixed valance states of cerium also exhibit antioxidant activities. As H2O2 is the most representative member of ROS, it was used in our experiment to verify the antioxidant activities of Ce-CNDs. At first, the cytotoxicity of pure H2O2 on L929 cells was measured, and the medianlethaldose of H2O2 is between 200 and 400 µM, thus 300 µM was further used to test the antioxidant properties of Ce-CNDs (Fig. 3b). Results demonstrate that the addition of Ce-CNDs can protect L929 cells from the strong oxidizing property of H2O2, with the optimal antioxidant concentration of 50 µg mL−1 Ce-CNDs (Fig. 3c). Notably, the cell viability of L929 cells after the treatment with both 50 µg mL−1 Ce-CNDs and 300 µM H2O2 almost recovered to 100%, indicating the attractive antioxidant ability of Ce-CNDs in the protection against H2O2-induced cell apoptosis.

In the proliferation stage of wound healing, cell migration and proliferation are both significant procedures for effective tissue regeneration and repair. To investigate the ability of Ce-CNDs to promote cell migration, in vitro wound scratch assay was performed, and the concentration of Ce-CNDs in this experiment was chosen to be 50 µg mL−1 based on the cytotoxicity and antioxidant assays. As demonstrated by Fig. 3e, the scratched area of L929 cells treated by Ce-CNDs close faster during 48 h of incubation with respect to the control group and the CNDs-treated group. The faster cell migration rate further indicates that Ce-CNDs exhibit superior biocompatibility and antioxidant properties. Furthermore, the healing rate of the scratched area is quantitatively calculated by Image J software for more intuitive description. As shown in Fig. 3d, the cell scratch coverage reaches 60.8% after 48 h of co-culture with Ce-CNDs, while that treated by none or pure CNDs only reached 49.4% and 40.4%, respectively. The in vitro wound scratch assay elucidates the effective promotion of cell migration ascribed to the doped cerium in Ce-CNDs, especially the mixed valence states of cerium, making Ce-CNDs promising material for wound healing.

Having established the excellent biocompatibility and the antioxidant capability, we introduced Ce-CNDs into L929 cells for in vitro bioimaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the cells pre-incubated with Ce-CNDs display blue, green and red luminescence upon the excitation of UV (340–380 nm), blue (460–500 nm) and green (517–563 nm) light, respectively. Contrarily, the cells without the treatment of Ce-CNDs have no visible luminescence under the same excitations (Fig. 4b). The luminescence of Ce-CNDs is mainly observed in the cytoplasmic area of cells, while the PL intensity in nucleus was much weaker. Notably, no photo-blinking and photobleaching appear during the fluorescence observation, indicating the superior photostability of Ce-CNDs and multifunctional applications in biomedical engineering.

To validate the efficacy of Ce-CNDs in vivo, we applied Ce-CNDs to the skin wound model and compared the healing efficiency of wounds treated with PBS, CNDs, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs, respectively. The representative images of wounds are shown in Fig. 5 a, which reveal that Ce-CNDs provide superiority in accelerating wound repair and tissue regeneration over other three groups. For quantitative analysis, the wound healing rates and the wound remaining areas are measured and calculated by Image J software as described in Fig. 5b, c, respectively.

From the first three days, wounds are susceptible to infection by bacteria, indicating the important role of antibacterial property in promoting the wound healing during this process. Compared with the groups treated by PBS and CNDs, both Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs treated wounds show better wound healing rate. The reason is that Ce(III) has already been verified to exhibit antibacterial ability [60], which endows both Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce-CNDs with certain antibacterial ability to resist the attack of bacteria. From day 5, the wounds enter the proliferation phase. At this stage, excessive inflammation will delay the wound healing. The reversible conversion between Ce(III) and Ce(IV) endows Ce-CNDs with unique antioxidant property, which contributes to the reduction of inflammatory response and further accelerates the wound healing process. The most obvious difference occurs on day 5, where the wound healing rate of Ce-CNDs can reach 65.6%, which is much higher than other three groups. As Ce(III) itself does not have anti-inflammatory property, the wound healing rate of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O treated group is similar to the CNDs group from day 5, but they are still higher than the PBS treated group. The rapid tendency of wound healing rate in Ce-CNDs treated group continued till day 15 after wound creation. Notably, on day 15 the wounds almost heal completely in the Ce-CNDs treated group, while full re-epithelialization still not achieves for the other groups, and pure CNDs and Ce(NO3)3·6H2O show relatively little promotion to the wound healing process compared with the PBS group.

To further evaluate the quality and maturity of the healing tissue, we conducted histopathological analysis on the tissue sections by H&E staining on days 3, 5, 8, 10 and 15 (Fig. 6 ). Generally, there are complete epithelial layer and compact collagen fiber in normal tissues, while the inflammatory cells are relatively rare. In contrast, the wound sections on day 3 show considerable inflammatory cells in all groups with blood vessels from the old epithelial layer. On day 5, there are still infiltration of inflammatory cells in all groups, but some new capillaries can be observed. The Ce-CNDs treated group shows fewer inflammatory cells and evidently more new blood vessels perpendicular to the wound section. During the wound healing process from day 5 to day 15, more hair follicles and blood vessels form and the number of inflammatory cells further decreases. On day 15, some inflammatory cells can still be observed in PBS, CNDs and Ce(NO3)3·6H2O treated groups. Instead, the Ce-CNDs treated wounds present fewer inflammatory cells and are covered with well-arranged compact collagen. H&E analysis indicates that Ce-CNDs have promoting effect of fibroplasia and angiogenesis, which further shows out why Ce-CNDs are effective in accelerating wound healing.

In summary, we have successfully developed a multifunctional Ce-CNDs by a simple and effective hydrothermal method as an integrated nanoplatform using in wound healing of SD rats for the first time. The introduction of cerium endows the as-prepared Ce-CNDs with attractive antioxidant capacity and superior performance in the acceleration of wound healing. Similar to pure CNDs, Ce-CNDs also show good hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, unique PL properties and excellent photostability under UV excitation, which are crucial for biomedical applications. Besides, the as-prepared Ce-CNDs possess special antibacterial activity under UV excitation which has not been discovered before. Overall, our synthesized Ce-CNDs provide novel design ideas for the integrated multifunctional nanoplatforms, showing potential developments for the visualization and treatment of other oxidative stress-related diseases.

Mengzhen Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Software, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xinyun Zhai: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Resources, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Tengfei Ma: Writing - review & editing, Data analysis. Yongkang Huang: Writing - review & editing, Data analysis. Chunhua Yan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Resources. Yaping Du: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Project administration, Resources.
